Q1: Honestly, my study routine was chaotic at best. I'd try to block out time in the evenings after work, but with 13 online courses and everything else going on, I was constantly playing catch-up. I'd make these elaborate schedules on Sunday nights, color-coded and everything, but by Wednesday they'd fall apart. I spent around 31 hours a week studying, which sounds like a lot, but spread across so many courses it never felt like enough. Most nights I'd sit down with good intentions and then just feel paralyzed by how much there was to do.
Q2: My pattern was completely driven by deadlines and panic, if I'm being honest. I'd tell myself I'd study every Tuesday and Thursday evening, but then something would come up or I'd be too exhausted. The weeks before major assignments were absolute chaos - I'd be up until 2am multiple nights in a row. When there weren't immediate deadlines, I'd let things slide, which just made the next crunch period worse. It was this terrible cycle I couldn't seem to break no matter how hard I tried.
Q3: Usually it was either panic about a deadline or getting an email notification that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which honestly just made my anxiety worse because there was always something I'd forgotten about. The peer support group I was assigned to would sometimes message me through the LMS, and that would prompt me to log in too. It wasn't really a proactive thing - more reactive, always feeling like I was putting out fires.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried so hard to make it routine, but it never stuck. There were maybe two weeks mid-semester where I was logging in daily and it started to feel normal, but then I got overwhelmed with assignments from multiple courses and everything fell apart. The LMS felt more like a source of stress than a helpful tool, so I'd avoid it when my anxiety was really bad. It's hard to build a routine around something that makes you feel terrible every time you open it.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually really helpful when I could engage with them - connecting with other students made me feel less alone in struggling. The reading materials were good because I could download them and read at my own pace, highlighting and taking notes. I also found the video lectures useful because I could pause and rewind when concepts weren't clicking. When peers would respond to my posts with encouragement or explanations, that was genuinely valuable for my learning and my mental state.
Q6: I barely touched the optional practice quizzes, which I know was probably a mistake given my exam score. I'd tell myself I'd get to them 'later' but later never came. Some of the supplementary readings felt like too much when I was already drowning in required material. The live discussion sessions were scheduled at times that didn't work with my schedule, so I missed most of those. I know I should have prioritized differently, but when you're overwhelmed, you just focus on what's screaming loudest.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the course or if it helped reduce my anxiety about upcoming assessments. Anything that counted toward my grade was automatically prioritized. I also valued activities where I felt like I was actually learning something that would stick, not just checking a box. The peer interactions were worth it because they made me feel supported, even when they weren't strictly 'productive' in an academic sense.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. Required activities came with this weight of obligation and fear of failure, which was stressful but at least motivated me to complete them. With optional stuff, my brain would immediately calculate whether I had the mental bandwidth, and the answer was usually no. It's sad because I know some of those optional resources might have actually helped me understand things better and reduced my stress in the long run.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I'd download PDFs and readings to my tablet so I could study during my commute or when the wifi was spotty. I have notebooks full of handwritten notes that I'd review away from my computer. I also used external YouTube videos to understand concepts that weren't clicking from the course materials alone. Sometimes I'd study with a peer from my support group in person, working through problems together at a coffee shop. A lot of my actual learning happened completely offline.
Q10: There were so many times I'd be lying in bed rereading my handwritten notes or going over flashcards I'd made, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd listen to downloaded lecture audio while doing chores or exercising. Sometimes I'd spend hours just thinking through concepts, trying to connect ideas in my head while walking or cooking. My study group would meet in person and we'd work through problems on a whiteboard - that was some of my most productive learning, but the LMS had no idea it was happening.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, taking notes, and then review just my notes for days afterward. I'd download readings and annotate them heavily, then refer back to those annotated versions instead of logging back in. It felt more efficient to have everything saved locally where I could access it without the anxiety of seeing all my overdue tasks. The LMS was like a one-time resource grab for me, then I'd retreat to my offline materials.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. I had multiple major assignments due across different courses within the same two-week period, and I just shut down. My anxiety got so bad I could barely look at my laptop without feeling sick. I stopped attending classes - my attendance dropped significantly during that time. I was sad and exhausted, and the peer support helped a little but I still felt like I was drowning. The more behind I got, the harder it was to face any of it.
Q13: Completely. When I was exhausted from trying to balance everything, I'd log into the LMS and just stare at the screen without actually processing anything. Confusion about assignment requirements would make me avoid starting them until the last possible minute. When work got demanding or family stuff came up, the LMS was the first thing to go because it felt like the most flexible obligation. The fatigue was real - sometimes I'd fall asleep at my desk trying to watch lectures.
Q14: So many times. I'd have a lecture video playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other deadlines or just feeling too anxious to focus. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. Sometimes I'd open readings and my eyes would move across the words but nothing would sink in. It was like going through the motions without actually being present, which made me feel even worse about myself.
Q15: Constantly. I'd avoid starting assignments because I was terrified of doing them wrong or not being good enough. The bigger and more important something was, the more I'd procrastinate because the stakes felt paralyzing. I knew the discussion posts were important for my grade, but some weeks I just couldn't bring myself to put my thoughts out there. It's this awful paradox where knowing something is important makes it harder to start, not easier. The anxiety just builds and builds.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage. I'd have these bursts of frantic activity in the 48 hours before something was due. My LMS usage probably looked like a flatline punctuated by spikes of panic. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I could overcome the paralysis. The problem was that last-minute work was never my best work, which hurt my grades and made me feel worse about myself, feeding the whole cycle.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Positive feedback would give me a temporary boost and make me want to engage more, but negative or critical feedback would send me into a spiral. I'd avoid checking my grades sometimes because I was too scared of what I'd see. When I did get constructive feedback, I'd try to apply it, but if too much time passed before the next similar assignment, I'd forget or lose momentum. The peer support feedback was gentler and easier to receive than instructor feedback.
Q18: The structure honestly overwhelmed me. Having 13 courses meant constant competing demands, and the LMS made it very visible how much I was falling behind. Weekly modules were helpful in theory but stressful when I couldn't keep up. I appreciated when courses had clear, predictable patterns, but some were all over the place with their expectations. The structure made it clear what I should be doing, which was helpful, but also made my failures very visible, which was not.
Q19: Courses with flexible deadlines or multiple submission attempts were much easier to engage with - they felt forgiving of my struggles. When everything was rigidly due at the same time with no extensions, I'd have to make impossible choices about what to prioritize. Clear rubrics helped reduce my anxiety about assignments. Discussion requirements that felt authentic rather than forced made me want to participate. The courses that acknowledged students might be struggling were easier to stay engaged with.
Q20: I think that's often anxiety and overwhelm. You're logging in, clicking around, trying to figure out what to do, maybe starting things and not finishing them. It could be someone who's lost and looking for guidance but not finding it. Or someone who's engaging with materials but then can't bring themselves to submit work because they're afraid it's not good enough. High activity doesn't mean productive activity - sometimes it's just panic browsing.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was okay and I passed, but my LMS engagement was low. I did a lot of learning offline - reading downloaded materials, studying with peers in person, using external resources. If someone is efficient with their time or learns well independently, they might not need to be constantly logged in. Or they might be like me, where anxiety keeps them away from the LMS but they're still putting in work elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when someone's active, what they're clicking on, whether they're meeting deadlines. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, the emotional state behind the activity, and all the offline learning. They can't tell if someone stared at a video for an hour while crying versus actively taking notes. They miss the peer study sessions, the handwritten notes, the thinking that happens away from the screen. They show behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious, when you want to understand rather than just pass. It's feeling like part of a learning community, not isolated and struggling alone. For me, engagement happened in moments - when a concept clicked, when a peer said something that helped, when I felt capable instead of overwhelmed. It's a mental and emotional state, not just a behavior.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was trying despite everything working against me. It can't show the tears, the panic attacks, the nights I couldn't sleep because I was so anxious about falling behind. It doesn't capture the genuine learning that happened in my peer support group or in my own head while reviewing notes. It can't show that my low attendance was about mental health, not laziness. It misses that I cared deeply about succeeding even when my behavior didn't reflect that.
